setup_guide.title: 📚 Setup Guide
setup_guide.art_backsweep_summary: Backward sweep of the handlebar in degrees.
setup_guide.art_backsweep_content: Backsweep describes the angle at which the ends of the handlebar are bent backwards (towards the rider). A higher backsweep provides a more natural wrist position and can reduce fatigue.
setup_guide.art_backsweep_tip: A higher backsweep (8-9°) is especially comfortable for the wrists on long descents.
setup_guide.art_barwidth_summary: Optimal handlebar width for control and comfort.
setup_guide.art_barwidth_content: Handlebar width affects control, leverage and comfort. Wider bars give more stability and leverage, but can be a hindrance on tight trails. The right width depends on shoulder width, riding style and intended use.
setup_guide.art_barwidth_tip: Cut the bar in 5mm increments from both sides. You can't glue cut material back on!
setup_guide.art_stemlength_summary: Stem length affects steering behavior and weight distribution.
setup_guide.art_stemlength_content: A shorter stem makes steering more direct and agile — ideal for steep trails. A longer stem provides more stable straight-line tracking and is more efficient when climbing. Modern MTBs use short stems (35–50mm).
setup_guide.art_stemlength_tip: Shorter stem + longer reach = modern, centered riding position.
setup_guide.art_riseupsweep_summary: Upward curvature and height increase of the handlebar.
setup_guide.art_riseupsweep_content: Rise describes the height difference between the clamp area and bar ends (0–35mm). Upsweep is the upward angle of the bar ends (typically 4–6°). Together they determine how high and ergonomic the grip position is.
setup_guide.art_riseupsweep_tip: More rise helps with an upright position and relieves the lower back.
setup_guide.art_leverpos_summary: Optimal position of brake and shift levers.
setup_guide.art_leverpos_content: Brake levers should be positioned so that you can brake with one finger (index finger) in the attack position without changing your grip. The lever should form a straight line with your forearm. Shift levers should be placed where they're reachable with your thumb.
setup_guide.art_leverpos_values: Lever angle: ~45° downward. Reach adjustment: Index finger at lever end.
setup_guide.art_leverpos_tip: Invest 10 minutes in lever setup — it makes a huge difference in comfort and control!
setup_guide.art_sag_summary: Compression under rider weight in resting position.
setup_guide.art_sag_content: SAG is the percentage of travel that the fork/shock compresses under the rider's body weight. It's the basis of any suspension setup and must be set first before adjusting rebound and compression.
setup_guide.art_sag_tip: Always measure in full riding gear and use the O-ring on the stanchion.
setup_guide.art_rebound_summary: Speed at which the suspension extends.
setup_guide.art_rebound_content: Rebound controls how fast the fork/shock returns to its initial position after a compression. Too fast = 'Kicking' (bike bucks you off). Too slow = 'Packing' (travel gets used up).
setup_guide.art_rebound_values: Rule of thumb: Ride off a curb, bike should return in 1 bounce.
setup_guide.art_rebound_tip: For DH: Rather a bit slower (more clicks in). For jumps: Rather a bit faster.
setup_guide.art_compression_content: Low-Speed Compression (LSC): Controls slow compression movements (bobbing, brake dive). High-Speed Compression (HSC): Controls fast hits (roots, drops). Not every fork has both.
setup_guide.art_compression_values: LSC: Start point in the middle, then adjust. HSC: A few clicks in, then test on a rough trail.
setup_guide.art_tokens_summary: Reduce the air chamber volume for more end progression.
setup_guide.art_tokens_content: Tokens/spacers reduce the volume of the air spring. This causes the pressure to rise more sharply towards the end of the stroke (more progressive curve). This prevents bottom-outs, but makes the mid-stroke 'firmer'.
setup_guide.art_tokens_values: DH/heavy riders: 2–3 tokens. Trail/light riders: 0–1 token
setup_guide.art_tokens_tip: Set SAG and Rebound first, then adjust tokens if you have bottom-outs.
setup_guide.art_airpressure_summary: Finding the right air pressure for your suspension.
setup_guide.art_airpressure_content: Air pressure in fork and shock determines spring rate. Starting point is always rider weight — most manufacturers provide pressure charts. Fine-tune via SAG: more pressure = less SAG = firmer suspension.
setup_guide.art_airpressure_values: Fork: Manufacturer chart as starting point. Shock: Manufacturer chart, then check SAG (25–35%).
setup_guide.art_airpressure_tip: Pump slowly and check via SAG. Small changes (5 PSI) make a big difference!
setup_guide.art_coilvsair_summary: Steel spring vs. air spring — pros and cons.
setup_guide.art_coilvsair_content: Air springs are lighter and easily adjustable (pump). Coil springs (steel) offer more sensitive response and more consistent performance in heat. Coil is popular for DH and enduro, air for trail and XC.
setup_guide.art_coilvsair_values: Coil: +200–400g extra weight. Air: Lighter, but seal friction.
setup_guide.art_coilvsair_tip: If you often bottom out or the response doesn't feel right, try coil!
setup_guide.art_bremsscheiben_summary: Diameter of the brake rotor affects braking power.
setup_guide.art_bremsscheiben_content: Larger rotors = more braking power and better heat dissipation on long descents. Standard sizes: 160mm (rear light), 180mm (standard), 200mm (DH front), 203mm+ (gravity).
setup_guide.art_bremsbelag_summary: Two pad types with different characteristics.
setup_guide.art_bremsbelag_content: Organic pads: Quieter, better modulation, wear faster, especially in the wet. Sintered pads (metal): Louder, more aggressive bite, more durable, better in wet and mud. Sintered recommended for DH and bikeparks.
setup_guide.art_bremsbelag_tip: Never mix organic and sintered pads! Rotor must be cleaned when switching.
setup_guide.art_bleeding_summary: Removing air from the brake system.
setup_guide.art_bleeding_content: Air in the brake line makes the pressure point spongy and reduces braking performance. Bleeding differs between Shimano (mineral oil) and SRAM (DOT fluid). Should be done at least once a year or when the pressure point feels soft.
setup_guide.art_bleeding_values: Shimano: Mineral oil. SRAM: DOT 5.1. Magura: Royal Blood. Never mix!
setup_guide.art_bleeding_tip: After bleeding: Pump firmly several times and check that the pressure point is solid.
setup_guide.art_bedding_summary: Properly bedding in new pads and rotors.
setup_guide.art_bedding_content: New brake pads need to be bedded in to achieve full braking performance. This transfers pad material evenly onto the rotor. Without bedding: Poor braking, squealing, uneven wear.
setup_guide.art_bedding_values: 20–30 stops from ~30 km/h to ~5 km/h. Never come to a complete stop!
setup_guide.art_bedding_tip: Best done on a clean, dry road — not on trails!
setup_guide.art_brakehose_summary: Cutting brake hoses to the right length.
setup_guide.art_brakehose_content: Brake hoses that are too long look messy and can snag on branches. When shortening, measure the line at the handlebar, cut cleanly, insert a new olive and barb, then re-bleed the brake.
setup_guide.art_brakehose_tip: Always use a new olive! Old olives deform and won't seal properly.
setup_guide.art_brakelever_summary: Setting the optimal lever reach and angle.
setup_guide.art_brakelever_content: The reach adjustment determines how far the lever is from the grip. Free stroke sets the dead travel before the bite point. Both should be set so you can brake with one finger (index finger).
setup_guide.art_brakelever_values: Reach: Index fingertip at lever end. Free stroke: Minimal dead travel.
setup_guide.art_brakelever_tip: Set up your levers in the attack position, not while standing at the handlebar!
setup_guide.art_chainlength_content: A chain that's too long can drop off, one that's too short can damage the derailleur and hanger. Method: Lay the chain over the largest cog and chainring (without threading through the derailleur), add 2 links.
setup_guide.art_chainlength_values: Rule of thumb: Largest cog + chainring + 2 links = done.
setup_guide.art_chainlength_tip: On full-suspension bikes: also account for full travel compression!
setup_guide.art_cassette_summary: Choosing the right cassette for your riding.
setup_guide.art_cassette_content: The cassette determines your gear range. 10-51T or 10-52T offer the widest range for MTB. For DH, smaller cassettes (7-speed) are often sufficient. Material: Steel is heavier but more durable, alloy/titanium is lighter.
setup_guide.art_cassette_tip: Always replace chain and cassette together — worn chains destroy new cassettes!
setup_guide.art_derailleur_content: A well-adjusted derailleur shifts quietly and precisely. Key adjustments: Limit screws (H and L limit the shifting range), B-screw (gap to the cassette), and cable tension (fine-tuning at the shifter).
setup_guide.art_derailleur_values: H/L screws: Guide pulley centered under smallest/largest cog. B-gap: 5–6mm.
setup_guide.art_derailleur_tip: Never shift under load! It damages chain, cassette and derailleur.
setup_guide.art_chainring_summary: The right chainring for your riding style.
setup_guide.art_chainring_content: Larger chainrings (34T+) = more top speed and pedaling efficiency. Smaller (28–30T) = easier climbing. The choice depends on fitness, terrain and cassette range. 1x systems typically use 30–34T.
setup_guide.art_chainring_tip: Going one size smaller (e.g. 30T instead of 32T) makes steep climbs much more comfortable.
setup_guide.art_chaincare_summary: Cleaning and lubricating the chain properly.
setup_guide.art_chaincare_content: A clean, well-lubricated chain lasts 2–3x longer and shifts more quietly. First clean (chain cleaner or brush), let dry, then lubricate. Wet lube for rainy/muddy conditions, dry lube for dry conditions.
setup_guide.art_chaincare_values: Cleaning: Every 2–3 rides. Lubing: After each cleaning or every 100–200km.
setup_guide.art_chaincare_tip: Wipe off excess lube — it attracts dirt and wears the chain faster!
setup_guide.art_tubeless_summary: Tires without inner tubes, using sealant.
setup_guide.art_tubeless_content: Tubeless setups allow for lower tire pressure (more grip), are more puncture-resistant (sealant closes small holes) and lighter. Requires tubeless-ready rims, special rim tape, valves, and sealant.
setup_guide.art_tubeless_values: Sealant: 60–90ml per tire. Refill every 3–6 months.
setup_guide.art_tubeless_tip: Use a compressor or booster pump to inflate the first time.
setup_guide.art_reach_summary: Horizontal distance from bottom bracket to top of head tube.
setup_guide.art_reach_content: Reach is the most important value when choosing a frame. It determines how stretched out you sit on the bike. Too short = cramped feeling, too long = too stretched.
setup_guide.art_reach_tip: Reach is more important than the calculated frame size. Always check the reach!
setup_guide.art_sattelhoehe_summary: Correct saddle height for efficient pedaling.
setup_guide.art_sattelhoehe_content: Saddle height is measured from the center of the bottom bracket to the top of the saddle. With a straight leg and heel on the pedal, the knee should be fully extended. With the ball of the foot on the pedal, there should be a slight bend.
setup_guide.art_sattelhoehe_values: Formula: Inseam × 0.885 = saddle height from bottom bracket
setup_guide.art_sattelhoehe_tip: With a dropper post, you ride downhill with a lowered saddle — but the maximum height must be correct!
setup_guide.art_druck_summary: Why is tire pressure so important?
setup_guide.art_druck_content: Tire pressure is the cheapest and most effective setup change. Less pressure = larger contact patch = more grip, but also a higher risk of bottom-outs and tire roll. Optimal: As low as possible, as high as necessary.
setup_guide.art_druck_values: Tubeless: Front 1.5-2.0 bar, Rear 1.7-2.2 bar. Tubes: +0.2-0.3 bar
setup_guide.art_druck_tip: Use the Pressure Bot feature for your personal recommendation!
setup_guide.art_wheelsize_summary: Pros and cons of different wheel sizes.
setup_guide.art_wheelsize_content: 29 inch: Better rollover, more stability at speed, faster. 27.5 inch: More agile, lighter, better for smaller riders and playful trails. Mullet (29 front, 27.5 rear): Combines rollover up front with agility in the rear.
setup_guide.art_wheelsize_values: 29: From ~165cm height. 27.5: Up to ~175cm or for playful bikes. Mullet: Enduro/DH.
setup_guide.art_wheelsize_tip: Try mullet if you're torn between sizes — the best of both worlds!
setup_guide.art_tirewidth_summary: How wide should your tire be?
setup_guide.art_tirewidth_content: Wider tires offer more grip and comfort, but are heavier and have more rolling resistance. The rim inner width determines which tire widths fit. Common MTB widths: 2.25 to 2.6 inches.
setup_guide.art_tirewidth_values: XC: 2.2–2.35. Trail: 2.35–2.5. Enduro/DH: 2.4–2.6. Rim 30mm = ideal for 2.4–2.6.
setup_guide.art_tirewidth_tip: Wider in front, narrower in rear — e.g. 2.5 front, 2.4 rear for less rolling resistance.
setup_guide.art_spoketension_summary: Even spoke tension for stable wheels.
setup_guide.art_spoketension_content: Uneven spoke tension leads to lateral wobble and spoke breakage. A tensiometer measures the tension. All spokes on one side should be as evenly tensioned as possible. The drive side is always higher tensioned than the non-drive side.
setup_guide.art_spoketension_values: Rear drive side: 100–130 kgf. Front/Rear non-drive: 60–90 kgf.
setup_guide.art_spoketension_tip: Get wheels trued by a pro if you lack experience — wrong tension makes it worse!
setup_guide.art_treadpattern_summary: Choosing the right tire for the terrain.
setup_guide.art_treadpattern_content: Aggressive tread with tall knobs = more grip in mud and loose soil, but more rolling resistance. Low, closely spaced knobs = faster on hardpack. Semi-slick rear saves weight and rolling resistance.
setup_guide.art_treadpattern_values: Mud: Open tread (Shorty, Muddy Mary). Hardpack: Tight (Ikon, Racing Ralph). All-round: Assegai, DHF.
setup_guide.art_treadpattern_tip: More aggressive front, faster rear — the Assegai/DHR II combo is a classic!
setup_guide.art_inserts_content: Tire inserts (e.g. CushCore, Tannus) protect the rim on impacts, allow lower pressure and improve damping. Downside: Extra weight (100–250g per tire) and harder installation.
setup_guide.art_inserts_values: Weight: 100–250g per insert. Pressure savings: 0.1–0.2 bar less possible.
setup_guide.art_inserts_tip: A must for bikepark and DH — for trail riding, often just a rear insert is enough.
setup_guide.art_stack_summary: Vertical distance from bottom bracket to top of head tube.
setup_guide.art_stack_content: Stack determines how upright you sit on the bike. High stack = upright, comfortable position. Low stack = stretched, sporty position. Stack and reach together define your basic position on the bike.
setup_guide.art_stack_tip: Stack can be adjusted by a few mm with spacers under the stem.
setup_guide.art_headangle_summary: Angle of the head tube relative to vertical.
setup_guide.art_headangle_content: A slack head angle (63–65°) provides stability at speed and on steep terrain. A steep angle (67–69°) makes steering more direct and agile. Modern enduro bikes are at 63–66°, trail bikes at 65–67°.
setup_guide.art_headangle_tip: An angleset headset can change the head angle by 0.5–2°.
setup_guide.art_bbheight_summary: Height of the bottom bracket above ground and drop relative to axle line.
setup_guide.art_bbheight_content: BB Drop is the distance between axle center and bottom bracket center. More drop = lower center of gravity = more stability, but higher risk of pedal strikes on rocks. BB Height is the actual ground clearance (depends on wheel size).
setup_guide.art_bbheight_tip: Low BB = more sense of security at speed, but watch out on technical uphills!
setup_guide.art_chainstay_summary: Distance from bottom bracket to rear axle affects handling.
setup_guide.art_chainstay_content: Short chainstays (420–435mm) make the rear more agile and easier to manual. Long chainstays (440–460mm) provide stability at speed and better climbing. Modern bikes use proportional chainstays based on frame size.
setup_guide.art_chainstay_tip: Short chainstays + long reach = modern, playful enduro handling.
setup_guide.art_seatangle_summary: Seat tube angle affects climbing efficiency.
setup_guide.art_seatangle_content: A steep seat angle (77–79°) positions the rider over the bottom bracket, enabling efficient pedaling and better climbing. Older geometries had slacker seat angles (73–75°), which placed the rider too far back.
setup_guide.art_seatangle_tip: A steep seat angle compensates for a long reach when climbing — pay attention to the combination!
setup_guide.art_dropper_summary: Lower and raise the saddle at the push of a button.
setup_guide.art_dropper_content: A dropper post is essential on an MTB. It allows you to lower the saddle for descents (more freedom of movement) and raise it for climbs. Travel ranges from 100–230mm depending on insertion depth and model.
setup_guide.art_dropper_tip: Choose as much travel as possible — more drop = more freedom downhill!
setup_guide.art_cleats_summary: Clipless vs. flat pedals — making the right choice.
setup_guide.art_cleats_content: Flat pedals: Simple, quick to dismount, ideal for beginners and DH/bikepark. Clipless (SPD): Fixed connection, more efficient pedaling, more secure hold on jumps. Many enduro riders use clipless pedals with multi-release cleats.
setup_guide.art_cleats_values: Flat pins: 8–10 per side. SPD release tension: Beginners light, experienced firmer.
setup_guide.art_cleats_tip: Start with flat pedals and only switch to clipless once your basic technique is solid!
setup_guide.art_saddle_summary: Finding the right saddle for you.
setup_guide.art_saddle_content: The right saddle is chosen based on sit bone width. Too narrow = pressure points, too wide = chafing. MTB saddles are shorter than road saddles and often have a center channel cutout for pressure relief.
setup_guide.art_saddle_values: Sit bone width: Narrow (<100mm): 130mm saddle. Medium: 143mm. Wide (>130mm): 155mm.
setup_guide.art_saddle_tip: Get your sit bone width measured at a bike shop — it saves a lot of trial and error!
setup_guide.art_pos_summary: The correct basic posture on the bike.
setup_guide.art_pos_content: Arms and legs slightly bent, elbows out, heels down, looking ahead (~3-5 meters), index finger on the brake. Weight centered. This position is the foundation for all riding situations on the trail.
setup_guide.art_pos_tip: Practice this position until it becomes automatic — it's the key to good MTB riding!
setup_guide.art_cleaning_summary: How to clean your bike properly and gently.
setup_guide.art_cleaning_content: Never point a pressure washer directly at bearings, seals or fork stanchions! Use a garden hose or bucket and sponge. Spray on bike cleaner, let it work, rinse off. Clean the chain separately with a chain cleaner.
setup_guide.art_cleaning_values: Clean after every muddy ride. In dry conditions: Every 3–5 rides is fine.
setup_guide.art_cleaning_tip: Pressure washing = death for bearings! Always use low pressure or a garden hose.
setup_guide.art_bearings_summary: Regular bearing maintenance prevents expensive damage.
setup_guide.art_bearings_content: Headset, bottom bracket, suspension linkage and hub bearings need regular maintenance. Clean bearings, check for play and rough spots, re-grease. Sealed bearings have a limited lifespan and may need replacement.
setup_guide.art_bearings_values: Check: Every 6 months. Grease: At every check. Replace: When rough/play ~yearly.
setup_guide.art_bearings_tip: Linkage bearings are most vulnerable — check them by wiggling the rear wheel sideways.
setup_guide.art_forkservice_summary: Regular service keeps the fork smooth.
setup_guide.art_forkservice_content: Lower-leg service (every 50–100h): Clean dust wipers, change bath oil. Full service (every 100–200h): Complete overhaul of air spring and damper cartridge. Without service, the fork becomes harsh and loses performance.
setup_guide.art_forkservice_values: Lower-leg: Every 50h or biannually. Full service: Every 200h or annually.
setup_guide.art_forkservice_tip: Between services: Regularly apply fork oil to the dust wipers!
setup_guide.art_shockservice_summary: The rear shock also needs regular service.
setup_guide.art_shockservice_content: Air-can service (every 50–100h): Check O-rings, lubricate, air chamber service. Full service (every 100–200h): Complete disassembly, oil change, seal replacement. Coil shocks: Change oil every 100–200h.
setup_guide.art_shockservice_values: Air-can: Every 50–100h. Full: Every 200h. Coil oil: Every 100–200h.
setup_guide.art_shockservice_tip: Watch for oil leaks on the shaft — that's a sign service is due!
setup_guide.art_carbon_content: Carbon is light and stiff, but sensitive to point loads and over-torquing. Always use a torque wrench! Carbon assembly paste (fine grit) prevents slipping at low torque. If you see visible cracks or dents: Replace immediately, carbon fails without warning!
setup_guide.art_carbon_values: Bar clamp: 4–6 Nm. Seatpost clamp: 4–5 Nm. Stem: 4–6 Nm.
setup_guide.art_carbon_tip: Never mount metal clamps on carbon without carbon paste — scratches = cracks!
setup_guide.art_torque_summary: Correct torque prevents damage and part loss.
setup_guide.art_torque_content: Every bolt on the bike has a specified torque (in Nm). Too tight = damage material (especially carbon!). Too loose = parts come loose while riding. A torque wrench is mandatory for every MTB owner.
setup_guide.art_torque_values: Stem: 5–6 Nm. Brake adapter: 6–8 Nm. Axle: 12–15 Nm. Pedals: 35–40 Nm.
setup_guide.art_torque_tip: Always tighten bolts in a cross pattern and gradually — don't fully tighten one bolt before moving to the next!
setup_guide.art_winterstorage_summary: How to store your bike properly for winter.
setup_guide.art_winterstorage_content: Before storage: Clean and dry the bike completely. Freshly lubricate the chain. Release air from forks/shocks (relieves seals). Tires at half pressure. No salt/moisture in the garage. Don't leave brake pads compressed.
setup_guide.art_winterstorage_tip: In spring before the first ride: Inflate pressure, check bolts, test brakes!
setup_guide.art_truing_summary: Fixing lateral and radial wheel wobble.
setup_guide.art_truing_content: An untrued wheel wobbles laterally (lateral runout) or hops (radial runout). Spoke tensions are adjusted with a spoke wrench to straighten the wheel. Requires practice and ideally a truing stand.
setup_guide.art_truing_tip: Always make quarter-turns on the spoke nipple — patience is key!
setup_guide.art_cables_summary: Maintaining and replacing shift cables and brake lines.
setup_guide.art_cables_content: Shift cables and their housings wear out and can degrade shifting performance. Lubricate new cables, cut housings to the correct length and use clean end caps. Check brake hoses for cracks. Internal routing: When replacing, make sure the cable is guided.
setup_guide.art_cables_values: Cable replacement: Yearly or when shifting feels sluggish. Brake hose: Every 2–3 years.
setup_guide.art_cables_tip: When replacing cables, use end caps on the housing ends — this prevents fraying!
setup_guide.art_headset_summary: The headset connects fork and frame — maintenance matters.
setup_guide.art_headset_content: The headset contains bearings that enable steering movement. Check regularly for play (pull front brake, rock bike back/forth — clicking = play). Clean bearings, grease and preload correctly. Worn bearings cause steering play and imprecise handling.
setup_guide.art_headset_values: Preload: Tighten until no play, but steering still turns freely. Check: Every 3–6 months.
setup_guide.art_headset_tip: Headset play is the most common unnoticed defect — check it every time you wash!

pressure_bot.title: 💨 Pressure Bot
pressure_bot.note_mullet: Mullet: Slightly more pressure front, slightly less rear
pressure_bot.note_insert: With an insert you can run ~0.1 bar less
pressure_bot.note_wet: In wet conditions: Less pressure = more grip!
pressure_bot.note_cold: Cold: Pressure drops approx. 0.05 bar per 10°C → inflate slightly more
